The paper introduces **Entropy-SGD**, an optimization method for deep networks that replaces minimization of the empirical loss with minimization of a **negative local-entropy objective** \( -F(x,\gamma) \) (Eq. (4)/(6)), intended to bias training toward **wide/flat valleys** that purportedly generalize better. The method uses a **two-loop procedure** (Alg. 1): an inner loop runs SGLD to approximate \(\langle x' \rangle\) under a locally-biased Gibbs distribution (Eq. (8)), and the outer loop updates parameters using the resulting local-entropy gradient (Eq. (7)). The paper provides (i) empirical motivation via Hessian spectra at trained solutions (Sec. 1, 5.1), (ii) a smoothness/stability-based generalization argument using Hardt et al. (2015) (Sec. 4.4, Eq. (9)) under strong assumptions, and (iii) experiments on MNIST/CIFAR-10 CNNs and PTB/character LSTMs showing comparable or slightly improved test performance, with notable reported speedup on RNNs (Sec. 5.4).

2) **Strengths (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- **Clear, principled objective and algorithmic derivation:** The local-entropy objective \(F(x,\gamma)\) is defined explicitly (Def. 1 / Eq. (4)), and the resulting gradient simplifies nicely to \(\gamma(x-\langle x'\rangle)\) (Eq. (7)), leading to an implementable nested-loop algorithm (Alg. 1).  
- **Good positioning relative to prior “flat minima / robust ensemble” work:** The discussion links to Baldassi et al. (2015/2016) and contrasts with smoothing/continuation methods (Sec. 2), clarifying that local entropy is not just convolutional smoothing and can prefer shallower-but-wider regions (Sec. 2, Fig. 2 discussion).  
- **Empirical breadth across architectures/datasets:** Experiments cover CNNs (MNIST LeNet-style; CIFAR-10 All-CNN-BN) and large RNNs (PTB-LSTM with ~66M parameters; Sec. 5.4.1), with repeated runs and error bars in several places (e.g., MNIST 5 runs; CIFAR-10 errors; PTB perplexity with ±).  
- **Useful ablation-style comparison to “just do SGLD”:** Appendix C.1 explicitly compares vanilla SGLD to Entropy-SGD on LeNet and All-CNN-BN, showing substantially worse errors for SGLD (e.g., 0.63% vs 0.50% on MNIST; 9.89% vs 7.81% on CIFAR-10), helping justify the specific local-entropy construction rather than generic sampling noise.

3) **Weaknesses (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- **Theoretical guarantee relies on assumptions the paper itself calls unrealistic:** Lemma 2 / Eq. (9) uses an eigenvalue exclusion condition (“no eigenvalue in \([ -2\gamma-c, c]\)”) that conflicts with the central empirical observation that ~94% eigenvalues are near zero (Sec. 1; Remark 4 explicitly acknowledges this). This weakens the interpretability of the generalization claim “Entropy-SGD generalizes better” (Sec. 4.4).  
- **Reproducibility gaps around key implementation choices:** While many hyperparameters are listed (Sec. 4.2, 5.2–5.4), some crucial details are underspecified or potentially confusing, e.g., the exact usage of the modified update “scale down the local entropy gradient by \(\gamma\)” changing line 7 to \(x \leftarrow x - \eta(x-\mu)\) (Sec. 4.3): it is unclear in each experiment whether Alg. 1 line 7 is used as written or the modified version.  
- **Fairness/interpretation of the “training time / speed-up” claims is not fully nailed down:** The paper introduces “effective epochs” by multiplying by \(L\) (Sec. 5.2 remark) to argue hardware-agnostic wall-clock comparisons, but speed claims (e.g., “2x speed-up over SGD on RNNs” in Abstract; “25 effective epochs vs. 55 epochs” in Sec. 5.4.1) still depend on constant factors (inner-loop overheads, optimizer differences, batch sizes, sequence unrolling costs) that are not fully reported.  
- **Limited direct evidence that Entropy-SGD actually finds “wider valleys” in the trained deep nets:** The motivation centers on Hessian spectra and “wide valleys,” but the experiments mainly report test error/perplexity and qualitative training curves; there is no direct quantitative flatness measure comparison between SGD vs Entropy-SGD solutions (beyond general statements like “lower training loss” in Sec. 6).

4) **Major Comments (numbered; focus on validity/significance/reproducibility; include actionable suggestions)**  

1. **Clarify and strengthen the empirical claim that Entropy-SGD finds flatter/wider solutions (central premise).**  
   - *Issue:* The paper argues wide valleys correlate with generalization (Sec. 1, 3) and proposes local entropy to bias optimization accordingly, but the experiments do not directly demonstrate that the *final solutions* from Entropy-SGD are flatter than those from SGD/Adam on the same setups. Sec. 5.1 shows Hessian spectra at minima found by existing optimizers (Adam/SGD), not Entropy-SGD vs baseline.  
   - *Why it matters:* Without direct evidence, improved generalization could be due to other confounders (changed dropout rates in MNIST experiments (Sec. 5.2), different momentum settings for RNNs (Sec. 5.4.2), different effective learning-rate behavior due to the two-loop scheme). Demonstrating “flatness” is important for validating the mechanism.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Add measurements comparing SGD/Adam vs Entropy-SGD solutions on at least one CNN and one RNN: e.g., dominant Hessian eigenvalues (via Hessian-vector products), sharpness proxies, or loss increase under parameter perturbations. The paper already discusses Hessian-vector products as viable (Sec. 5.1), so this seems feasible.  
   - *Essential?* Not strictly essential for publication if results are strong, but it would significantly strengthen the causal story and novelty claim.

2. **Make the training-time comparisons more rigorous and less dependent on “effective epochs” abstraction.**  
   - *Issue:* The “effective epochs = epochs × L” argument (Sec. 5.2) is a reasonable normalization for gradient evaluations, but the paper also makes wall-clock claims (Abstract; Sec. 5.4.1) while using different outer/inner optimizers in some cases (Sec. 4.2) and changing momentum values specifically for RNNs (Sec. 5.4.2). Additionally, Entropy-SGD performs fewer *outer* updates, which can change overhead and convergence behavior beyond raw gradient eval counts.  
   - *Why it matters:* Speedups are a headline claim (notably for RNNs), and readers will want to know whether gains persist under matched compute budgets and comparable tuning effort.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Report (a) number of forward/backward passes, (b) batch sizes, (c) actual wall-clock time on stated hardware (even if one setup), and (d) total gradient evaluations until reaching a target validation metric. Alternatively, run baselines with matched gradient-evaluation budgets (“same number of minibatch gradient computations”) and compare validation curves.  
   - *Essential?* Important for the “trains much more quickly” claim; at least one compute-matched comparison would be valuable.

3. **Theoretical section: better align the generalization claim with the (acknowledged) violation of assumptions.**  
   - *Issue:* Sec. 4.4 derives improved stability via smoothness (Lemma 2 + Hardt et al. Thm. 3) yielding Eq. (9), but the eigenvalue-gap assumption is contradicted by the observed near-zero eigenvalues (Sec. 1, 5.1) and Remark 4 admits this is unrealistic. As written, the bound may be interpreted as stronger support than warranted.  
   - *Why it matters:* Overstating theory can undermine trust; also, the analysis uses a saddle-point approximation in Appendix B (deriving \((\nabla^2 f(x)+\gamma I)^{-1}\nabla f(x)\)), which introduces additional approximations not fully discussed in the main text.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Reframe Eq. (9) more explicitly as *heuristic* guidance; discuss the role of near-zero Hessian directions in Entropy-SGD (which are central empirically). If possible, add an empirical stability proxy (e.g., sensitivity to removing a data point is hard, but sensitivity to minibatch noise / initialization could be shown) to connect to the stability narrative without relying on global smoothness.  
   - *Essential?* Not essential to accept the paper (the algorithm/experiments may stand), but the claims should be toned and clarified.

4) **Minor Comments (numbered; clarity, missing details, presentation, small issues)**  

1. **Specify per-experiment whether the modified outer update \(x \leftarrow x-\eta(x-\mu)\) (Sec. 4.3) is used.** The algorithm box (Alg. 1) shows \(x \leftarrow x-\eta\gamma(x-\mu)\), while Sec. 4.3 suggests dividing by \(\gamma\) in practice; it is unclear which was used in MNIST/CIFAR/RNN experiments.  
2. **Dropout changes confound MNIST comparisons:** In Sec. 5.2, Entropy-SGD uses reduced dropout probabilities (0.15/0.25) compared to the baseline “dropout 0.5 after each layer.” Even though performance is comparable, this complicates attributing differences to the optimizer. A short justification or an additional control would help.  
3. **Hessian vs Fisher proxy discussion:** In Sec. 5.1(iv), the diagonal empirical Fisher is used as a proxy for Hessian eigenvalues. The text notes equality for negative log-likelihood “in a neighborhood of a local minimum,” but using only the diagonal is a strong simplification; a brief caveat about what conclusions are still supported would improve clarity.

5) **Questions for Authors (3-6 questions that would clarify or strengthen the work)**  
1. In each experiment (MNIST/CIFAR/PTB/War&Peace), did you update the outer loop using Alg. 1 line 7 (\(\eta\gamma(x-\mu)\)) or the modified rule in Sec. 4.3 (\(\eta(x-\mu)\))?  
2. For MNIST (Sec. 5.2), can you report results when keeping dropout identical between baseline and Entropy-SGD (both at 0.5), to isolate the optimizer’s effect?  
3. How sensitive are results to the exponential averaging parameter \(\alpha=0.75\) (Alg. 1 line 6) and to the number of inner steps \(L\)? Do you observe failure modes when \(L\) is small (e.g., 1–2) or large?  
4. For the speedup claims on PTB-LSTM (Sec. 5.4.1), can you provide actual wall-clock times and hardware details, and/or a comparison under a matched number of minibatch gradient computations?  
5. Do solutions found by Entropy-SGD empirically have lower “sharpness” than SGD solutions (e.g., top Hessian eigenvalues, perturbation-based sharpness, or loss increase under parameter noise) on at least CIFAR-10 All-CNN-BN?

6) **Recommendation**  
**Weak Accept**. The paper proposes a neat and well-motivated optimization objective (local entropy) with an implementable nested SGLD/SGD algorithm (Alg. 1) and demonstrates competitive results across CNNs and large RNNs, including a compelling practical improvement on PTB. The main limitations are the gap between the “wide valleys” mechanism and the empirical evidence presented, plus some clarity/reproducibility issues (notably around the \(\gamma\)-scaled update and compute comparisons). Addressing these would make the work substantially stronger, but the core idea and results seem publishable.

1) **Summary (1 paragraph; briefly describe what the paper does and its main claims/results)**  
The paper proposes **Deep Multi-Task Representation Learning (DMTRL)**, a framework for multi-task learning in deep neural networks that **learns layer-wise sharing structure automatically** by generating each layer’s task-specific weights via **tensor factorisation** (DMTRL-LAF, DMTRL-Tucker, DMTRL-TT). Conceptually, it generalises classic matrix-factorisation MTL (e.g., \(W=LS\)) to the case where DNN parameters across tasks form higher-order tensors (including convolution kernels), enabling “soft” sharing rather than the user-defined “hard share then fork” strategy. Experiments on **MNIST (homogeneous 10 one-vs-all tasks)**, **AdienceFaces (heterogeneous gender + age)**, and **Omniglot (50 alphabets, heterogeneous outputs)** show DMTRL (especially Tucker/TT variants) generally outperform STL and are comparable to or better than the best cross-validated user-defined sharing baseline (UD-MTL), particularly in low-data regimes; the paper also introduces a cosine-similarity-based measure \(\rho\) (Eq. 9) to quantify learned sharing.

2) **Strengths (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- **Clear, principled generalisation of matrix MTL to deep nets:** The paper explicitly connects classic \(W=LS\) (Eq. 1–2) to tensor factorisations (Tucker Eq. 3–5; TT Eq. 6–8) and then to deep-layer weight generation (Section 3.3), making the conceptual bridge easy to follow.  
- **Applies to both homogeneous and heterogeneous MTL:** The framework is evaluated on heterogeneous settings where output dimensions differ (AdienceFaces gender vs age; Omniglot alphabets with varying class counts), and the paper acknowledges the output layer must be unshared in that case (footnote in Section 3.3).  
- **Empirical comparison against a strong deep baseline:** Instead of only comparing to shallow linear MTL, the paper constructs a **cross-validated “user-defined” hard-sharing Deep-MTL baseline** by searching “share first \(N\) layers” (e.g., MNIST chooses \(N=3\); Adience chooses \(N=5\); Omniglot chooses \(N=3\)), which is a reasonable and grounded competitor.  
- **Pragmatic rank-selection/initialisation story and code availability:** Implementation is in TensorFlow with a GitHub link (Section 4, footnote 4). The rank selection via STL pretraining + SVD threshold (10% reconstruction error) reduces per-layer tuning burden to a single interpretable knob.

3) **Weaknesses (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- **Key training details are underspecified for reproducibility:** While architectures and losses are described (e.g., hinge loss for MNIST; tanh for Omniglot), the paper does not specify optimizers, learning rates, batch sizes, epochs, regularisation/weight decay, early stopping, or data preprocessing/augmentation—details that materially affect deep CNN results.  
- **The “soft sharing” claim lacks targeted ablations isolating where gains come from:** DMTRL is compared to STL and UD-MTL, but there is limited analysis separating effects of (i) factorised parameterisation/regularisation, (ii) rank selection via STL+SVD, and (iii) the ability to interpolate sharing per layer. Appendix B discusses capacity vs UD-MTL(-Large), but not other mechanisms.  
- **Evaluation reporting is incomplete in the extracted text:** Results are primarily referenced via figures (Fig. 2–4) that are not included here, and there are few concrete numeric results in the main text (beyond a “below 6%” statement for MNIST with 1000 points and a qualitative description of trends). This makes it harder to assess effect sizes and variance.  
- **The learned-sharing metric \(\rho\) (Eq. 9) is somewhat ad hoc and may be sensitive to transformations:** Taking absolute values and softmax-normalising columns of \(S\) before cosine similarity may obscure sign structure and scale, and it is not validated against alternative notions of sharing or correlated with performance.

4) **Major Comments (numbered; focus on validity/significance/reproducibility; include actionable suggestions)**  

1. **Reproducibility: missing core optimisation and training protocol details (essential).**  
   - *Issue:* Section 4 gives architectures and some losses, but omits key elements (optimizer, LR schedule, batch size, number of updates/epochs, weight decay/dropout, initialisation specifics beyond rank selection, whether layers are jointly trained end-to-end from the factorised parameters, etc.). For Omniglot, the split is random “5…90% per class,” but it is not clear how many repetitions are used (it says “repeatedly pick at random,” but not how many trials), and whether task sampling is balanced during training across 50 alphabets.  
   - *Why it matters:* The reported improvements, especially in low-data regimes (where the paper claims DMTRL-TT/Tucker beat UD-MTL on MNIST when data is “very small”), can be sensitive to these settings.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Add a concise “Training Details” subsection per dataset: optimizer (e.g., SGD/Adam), learning rates and schedules, batch sizes, epochs/early stopping criteria, any augmentation, and the task batching strategy (how gradients from tasks are aggregated; whether tasks are sampled uniformly). Also specify the exact number of random repeats for Omniglot fractions, analogous to the “repeat 5 times” stated for MNIST.  
   - *Essential for publication?* Yes—at least the optimisation protocol and evaluation repetition counts should be clearly stated.

2. **Clarify what is being compared: factorised parametrisation vs learned sharing (important).**  
   - *Issue:* DMTRL’s advantages are attributed to “learning where and how much to share” (Section 3.3), but the method also changes the model class by introducing factorised parameter generation (Tucker/TT), which can act as a strong inductive bias/regulariser independent of cross-task sharing. The UD-MTL baseline searches only a restricted family (“share first \(N\) layers”) and is acknowledged as not exhaustive (footnote 5), so it is unclear whether gains come from (a) broader sharing structures, (b) soft vs hard sharing, or (c) simply the factorised parameterisation.  
   - *Why it matters:* This affects the paper’s central claim that it “significantly reduc[es] the space of DNN design choices” while improving accuracy; reviewers will want evidence that the benefits arise from *learned sharing structure* rather than only *compression/regularisation*.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Provide an ablation such as: (i) DMTRL with \(S=I_T\) (no sharing) but still factorised (if meaningful), (ii) DMTRL where \(S\) is constrained to the UD-MTL form (all-ones row / shared) to mimic hard sharing within the same parametrisation, or (iii) compare against a “soft sharing” baseline like weighted parameter averaging or cross-stitch-style mixing (even just on one dataset). If adding new baselines is out-of-scope, at minimum add analysis showing that learned \(\rho\) (Eq. 9) changes systematically with data fraction and correlates with performance improvements.  
   - *Essential for publication?* Important but not strictly essential if the empirical improvements are strong; however, at least one targeted ablation would substantially strengthen the core claim.

3. **Statistical reporting: variance/significance and numeric tables (important).**  
   - *Issue:* The text states MNIST experiments repeat 5 times per fraction and Omniglot sampling is repeated, but results are described qualitatively (“outperform,” “comparable”) and appear primarily in Fig. 2–4. There are no confidence intervals, standard deviations, or statistical tests reported in the text, despite stochasticity from sampling fractions and training.  
   - *Why it matters:* Especially for statements like “UD-MTL is not consistently better than STL” on Adience and “UD-MTL reduces performance when the training set is bigger” (negative transfer claim), variability could change conclusions.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Add mean±std (or 95% CI) over repeats for each method at key data fractions, ideally in a table. For Adience, clarify whether 5-fold CV used for architecture selection is separate from the final train/test evaluation and whether final runs are repeated with different seeds.  
   - *Essential for publication?* Important; numerical summaries with variance would make claims much more credible.

4) **Minor Comments (numbered; clarity, missing details, presentation, small issues)**  

1. **Terminology/notation consistency:** The paper uses \(S\) both as the Tucker core tensor (Eq. 3–5) and as the task-coefficient matrix in matrix factorisation (Eq. 1–2) and later as “S-like” objects across variants (Section 4.3). Consider renaming (e.g., \(G\) for Tucker core) to reduce confusion.  
2. **UD-MTL search space description:** Footnote 5 acknowledges the searched architectures are not exhaustive; it would help to briefly justify why the restricted “share first \(N\)” family is sufficient for a strong baseline in each dataset, or provide a small additional alternative pattern tried.  
3. **Bias parameters not shared:** Section 4 states “bias terms are not shared.” A brief rationale (or an ablation showing it doesn’t matter) would help, since biases can encode task-specific offsets and may interact with sharing.

5) **Questions for Authors (3-6 questions that would clarify or strengthen the work)**  

1. What optimizer(s), learning rates, batch sizes, and stopping criteria are used for MNIST/Adience/Omniglot, and are they identical across STL, UD-MTL, and DMTRL variants?  
2. For DMTRL training, do you (a) explicitly synthesise full \(W\) tensors each forward pass as described in Section 3.3, or (b) use implicit contractions to avoid materialising \(W\)? What is the computational/memory overhead relative to UD-MTL?  
3. In rank selection (Section 4), is the “10% max reconstruction error” applied per-layer and per-decomposition step? How sensitive are results to this threshold (e.g., 5%, 20%)?  
4. For Omniglot, how many random repetitions are used per training fraction, and how is training balanced across 50 tasks with different numbers of classes?  
5. In Eq. 9’s sharing measure \(\rho\), why use abs + softmax before cosine similarity? Did you try alternatives (e.g., cosine on raw columns; correlation; entropy of softmaxed coefficients), and do conclusions about “share more in lower layers” hold?

6) **Recommendation (one of: Accept / Weak Accept / Weak Reject / Reject) + 2-3 sentence justification**  
**Weak Accept.** The paper presents a compelling and fairly general idea—casting deep multi-task parameter sharing as tensor factorisation-based weight generation across layers—and demonstrates it on both homogeneous and heterogeneous MTL settings with a reasonable user-defined hard-sharing baseline. The main limitations are incomplete reporting of optimisation/evaluation details and a lack of targeted ablations disentangling “learned sharing” from “factorised parametrisation/regularisation,” but these seem addressable without changing the core approach.

1) **Summary (1 paragraph; briefly describe what the paper does and its main claims/results)**  
The paper proposes *self-ensembling* methods for semi-supervised learning that enforce prediction consistency under stochastic regularization and input augmentation. It introduces two implementations: the **Π-model** (Algorithm 1), which matches predictions from two stochastic passes of the same input, and **temporal ensembling** (Algorithm 2), which forms targets by exponentially averaging predictions across epochs with bias correction. The authors report state-of-the-art results on SVHN and CIFAR-10 in low-label regimes (e.g., SVHN 500 labels: 7.05% non-augmented and 5.12% with augmentation; CIFAR-10 4000 labels: 16.55% non-augmented and 12.16% with augmentation per Abstract/Section 3), show benefits from adding unlabeled Tiny Images data for CIFAR-100 (Section 3.3), and demonstrate increased tolerance to label noise (Section 3.5).

2) **Strengths (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- Clear, simple objective and algorithms: both Π-model and temporal ensembling are described with explicit pseudocode (Algorithms 1–2) and a concrete loss combining labeled cross-entropy with an unlabeled consistency MSE term.  
- Temporal ensembling is a neat efficiency improvement: the paper motivates and implements it as a ~2× speedup over Π-model by avoiding the second forward pass per minibatch while also reducing target noise via averaging over epochs (Section 2.2).  
- Strong empirical performance on standard SSL benchmarks: the paper reports substantial error reductions on SVHN and CIFAR-10 in limited-label settings and provides mean/std over **10 runs** (Section 3), which is good practice for stability.  
- Thoughtful discussion of augmentation and fair comparison issues: the authors explicitly separate “non-augmented” vs “standard augmentations” and discuss why comparing to Sajjadi et al. (2016b) is difficult due to fractional max pooling and aggressive augmentations (Sections 3.1–3.2).

3) **Weaknesses (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- Some key experimental details are referenced but not present in the provided text (e.g., “Table 1/2/3/4/5/6” and “Figure 2” are cited for results and architecture, but not included here), making it hard to fully assess baselines, exact numeric comparisons, and architectural capacity from the text alone (Section 3; Appendix A references Table 5).  
- Limited ablation isolating *which components* drive gains (dropout vs specific augmentations vs ramp-up schedule vs choice of MSE consistency): while the paper notes “Squared difference gave slightly but consistently better results” (footnote in Section 2.2) and emphasizes slow ramp-up importance (Section 2.1; Appendix A), there is no systematic breakdown of these factors in the provided text.  
- Potential evaluation/validity concern for CIFAR-100 + Tiny Images: the authors state they “did not explicitly prevent overlap” and that “approximately a third of the CIFAR-100 training and test images [are] present as unlabeled inputs in the extra set” (Appendix A). This can confound interpretation of improvements from extra unlabeled data because the unlabeled pool may contain test images.  
- Reproducibility gaps around dataset sampling and label selection: the paper states it does not stratify minibatches to ensure labeled examples appear (Appendix A), and for Tiny Images it samples “50k unlabeled inputs… randomly on each epoch” and updates only some rows of Z (Section 3.3). The exact sampling protocol and random seed handling for these aspects are not fully specified in the main text.

4) **Major Comments (numbered; focus on validity/significance/reproducibility; include actionable suggestions)**  

1. **Potential data leakage / transductive contamination in CIFAR-100 + Tiny Images experiments**  
   - *Issue:* Appendix A explicitly notes that the extra unlabeled Tiny Images set overlaps with CIFAR-100, including “approximately a third of the CIFAR-100 training and test images being present as unlabeled inputs in the extra set.” If test images are used (even unlabeled) during training, the reported gains in Section 3.3/“Table 4” could partially reflect transductive exposure rather than general semi-supervised learning.  
   - *Why it matters:* This affects the validity of the claim that “random natural images” improve accuracy and that category restriction does not help. Including test images can inflate performance and undermines comparability to prior work that assumes no test-time exposure.  
   - *Actionable fix:* Re-run (or at least report) results with **explicit de-duplication** against both CIFAR-100 training *and test* sets (ideally via hash matching of raw images). If de-duplication is expensive, at minimum provide an additional table separating (i) fully de-overlapped extra set and (ii) overlapped extra set to quantify the effect.  
   - *Essential?* Yes for the CIFAR-100+extra-data claim; the core method on CIFAR-10/SVHN likely stands regardless, but the extra-data conclusions need this.

2. **Need for clearer, component-level ablations to support central design choices**  
   - *Issue:* The paper attributes performance to the combination of dropout + stochastic augmentation + consistency loss, and emphasizes ramp-up scheduling (Section 2.1; Appendix A), but provides limited ablations in the provided text. For example: How much does performance drop without dropout? Without augmentation? With different α in temporal ensembling (fixed to 0.6 in Appendix A)? How sensitive is wmax (varies widely across settings in Appendix A)?  
   - *Why it matters:* Since the method is simple, understanding *what matters* is crucial for adoption and for validating that improvements are not due to tuning/architecture rather than the self-ensembling principle. It also helps practitioners apply temporal ensembling robustly.  
   - *Actionable fix:* Add an ablation section (even small) reporting at least: (i) Π-model/temporal ensembling with and without standard augmentations, (ii) with reduced/removed dropout, (iii) α sweep (e.g., 0.0, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9), (iv) ramp-up duration sensitivity (e.g., 20/80/160 epochs). Report mean±std over seeds as done elsewhere (Section 3).  
   - *Essential?* Important but not strictly essential for acceptance if space is limited; however, at least one sensitivity analysis (α and ramp-up) would significantly strengthen the paper.

3. **Clarify evaluation protocol and fairness of baseline comparisons**  
   - *Issue:* The paper states “we believe that our comparison methods do not use input augmentation” (Section 3), and then reports both non-augmented and augmented results. However, the baselines’ exact augmentation/regularization/training recipe is not fully specified here, and the discussion around Sajjadi et al. (2016b) highlights large differences in augmentation strength and fractional max pooling (Sections 3.1–3.2).  
   - *Why it matters:* Semi-supervised benchmarks are sensitive to augmentation and model capacity; without a clear apples-to-apples protocol, it’s harder to interpret “new records” as algorithmic gains rather than recipe differences.  
   - *Actionable fix:* For each compared method in Tables 1–2 (not shown here), explicitly annotate whether augmentation, extra data, or architectural changes are used. Also, provide your own supervised-only baselines *matched* to the SSL setting (same architecture, same augmentations, same training length), which you partially do (e.g., CIFAR-10 baseline 34.85% with 4000 labels in Section 3.1) but should be consistently tabulated alongside SSL numbers.  
   - *Essential?* Some clarification is essential; full reproduction of others’ setups is not, but clearer framing of what is/isn’t comparable would help.

4) **Minor but important reproducibility clarifications for temporal ensembling state (Z) updates and sampling**  
   - *Issue:* Temporal ensembling maintains per-example Z across epochs (Algorithm 2), but Section 3.3 describes a variant where only 50k unlabeled Tiny Images are used per epoch and “in temporal ensembling, after each epoch we updated only the rows of Z that corresponded to inputs used on that epoch.” This is a non-trivial deviation from the canonical algorithm and could affect behavior.  
   - *Why it matters:* Researchers attempting to reproduce or extend the method need a precise definition of how Z is handled when examples are not seen every epoch (staleness, bias correction using t, etc.).  
   - *Actionable fix:* Specify precisely: whether bias correction uses global epoch t or per-example count; how Z is initialized/updated for examples first seen late; whether unlabeled sampling is with/without replacement across epochs; and whether labeled examples are always included (seems yes: “all 50k labeled inputs” in Section 3.3).  
   - *Essential?* Not essential for the core CIFAR-10/SVHN story, but important for the extra-data setting and for faithful replication.

5) **Minor Comments (numbered; clarity, missing details, presentation, small issues)**  

1. In Algorithm 2 notation, clarify the scope of variables `z` vs `zi∈B` when updating `Z ← αZ + (1−α)z` at epoch end; the text implies full-dataset predictions, but minibatch evaluation yields partial vectors unless cached. A brief clarification would avoid confusion.  
2. The paper mentions “random horizontal flips need to be done independently for each epoch in temporal ensembling” and that Π-model can “randomize once per a pair of evaluations” with ~0.5 pp effect (Section 3.1). It would help to formalize this: is the augmentation for the consistency target meant to be *matched* or *independent* across teacher/student?  
3. Appendix A notes preliminary instability with 250 labels in SVHN and suggests lowering Adam β2 (0.99 vs 0.999). Consider reporting whether the main SVHN results required such interventions, and whether optimization stability is a general concern.

6) **Questions for Authors (3-6 questions that would clarify or strengthen the work)**  

1. For CIFAR-100 + Tiny Images (Section 3.3; Appendix A): can you provide results with **test-set overlap removed** from the unlabeled pool, and quantify how much of the reported gain remains?  
2. In temporal ensembling (Algorithm 2), is α fixed at 0.6 purely by tuning, and how sensitive are results to α across CIFAR-10 and SVHN?  
3. For the unsupervised loss scaling, Appendix A uses `wmax · M/N` and widely different `wmax` across experiments (e.g., 30, 100, 1000, 3000). How should practitioners set `wmax` in new datasets, and what is the stability range before degeneracy noted in Section 2.1 occurs?  
4. In Section 3, you report mean/std over 10 seeds. Are the labeled subsets (e.g., 4000 labels in CIFAR-10, 500/1000 in SVHN) fixed across runs or re-sampled per seed?  
5. For the label-noise experiment (Section 3.5, Figure 2), what exact corruption process is used (uniform random label among C classes?), and is corruption applied only to labeled subset or to all training examples?

7) **Recommendation (one of: Accept / Weak Accept / Weak Reject / Reject) + 2-3 sentence justification**  
**Accept.** The paper presents a simple, compelling idea (self-ensembling via consistency across stochastic perturbations) with a particularly elegant temporal ensembling formulation (Algorithm 2) and strong reported gains on CIFAR-10/SVHN with multiple seeds. The main caveat is the CIFAR-100+Tiny Images overlap issue (Appendix A), which should be corrected/clarified, but it does not undermine the core method contributions and benchmark results on the standard SSL tasks.

1) Summary (1 paragraph; briefly describe what the paper does and its main claims/results)  
The paper tackles supervised classification with noisy training labels by explicitly modeling the label-noise process inside a neural network. Building on the latent-variable formulation in Eq. (1)–(2), it proposes adding a “noise adaptation” softmax layer on top of the usual true-label softmax so that training directly maximizes the noisy-label likelihood in Eq. (12), and argues this matches EM’s objective for the feature-independent noise case (s-model; Eq. (10)–(13)) while avoiding iterative EM retraining. The approach is extended to feature-dependent noise (c-model; Eq. (8)–(9)), and a sparsified variant is proposed to reduce the quadratic-in-classes cost by connecting each true label to only its top‑l likely noisy labels based on an initial confusion matrix (Section 3). Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR‑100 with synthetically permuted label noise report that both s-model and c-model outperform a baseline and the methods of Reed et al. (2014), with bootstrapped confidence intervals (Section 4, Figures 2–4).

2) Strengths (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)  
- Clean probabilistic grounding: the paper clearly derives the noisy-label likelihood (Eq. (2)) and shows how the proposed network optimizes the same objective (Eq. (12)–(13)), tying the method to EM while avoiding explicit E/M alternation.  
- Practical architectural simplicity: the key change is “simply adding another softmax output layer” (Conclusion; Section 3), making it easy to integrate into standard training via backprop.  
- Addresses an important limitation of common noisy-label models by introducing a feature-dependent noise model (c-model; Eq. (8)–(9)) rather than assuming conditional independence of noise from features (Section 1 and 3).  
- Includes scalability discussion and a concrete sparsification heuristic (top‑l connections per class) with an empirical check on CIFAR‑100 showing little performance loss (Section 3; Figure 4).

3) Weaknesses (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)  
- Experimental noise model is limited to a specific synthetic “predefined permutation” corruption (MNIST and CIFAR‑100; Section 4). This does not test more realistic class-dependent confusion or instance-dependent noise patterns that the method is partly motivated to handle (especially the c-model).  
- Reproducibility gaps for the c-model and CIFAR‑100 setup: while MNIST hyperparameters are detailed (2 hidden layers 500/300, dropout 0.5, Adam defaults, batch size 256), CIFAR‑100 architecture/training details are only high-level (“two convolutional layers… followed by two fully connected layers”), and no specific hyperparameters/regularization/epochs are provided (Section 4).  
- Limited ablation/diagnosis: key design choices—initialization strategy (uniform-noise vs confusion-matrix init), s-model→c-model initialization, and sparsification parameter l—are described but not systematically ablated (Section 3).  
- The evaluation protocol description around uncertainty is unclear: bootstrapping is described as drawing “N = 10 samples … from the N available samples” (Section 4), which is confusing and makes it hard to interpret the confidence intervals without knowing what constitutes an “available sample” (runs? noise seeds?).

4) Major Comments (numbered; focus on validity/significance/reproducibility; include actionable suggestions)  

1. **Experimental noise is narrow relative to the paper’s claims (especially instance-dependent noise).**  
   - *Issue:* Both MNIST and CIFAR‑100 experiments generate noise by “changing some of the labels… according to a predefined permutation” with probability p (Section 4). This is a structured but still fairly simple corruption; it does not reflect common annotation confusions (non-permutation, asymmetric), nor does it exercise the c-model’s feature dependence in a targeted way.  
   - *Why it matters:* The paper motivates c-model by arguing noise can depend on misleading features (Section 1), yet the synthetic corruption described is not explicitly instance-dependent. If the injected noise is independent of x, improvements of c-model over s-model could be incidental rather than demonstrating the intended advantage.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Add at least one additional noise setting: (i) asymmetric class-dependent confusion (non-permutation, with a few dominant confusions per class), and (ii) instance-dependent noise where corruption probability increases for “hard” examples (e.g., low margin under a clean model) to directly test Eq. (8). Report s-model vs c-model under these.  
   - *Essential?* Not strictly essential for acceptance if results are strong, but important to substantiate the claimed motivation and to clarify when c-model helps.

2. **Insufficient implementation/training details for full reproducibility on CIFAR‑100 and for the c-model.**  
   - *Issue:* CIFAR‑100 is described without key details (filter sizes/channels, pooling specifics, dropout/weight decay, learning rate schedule, number of epochs, early stopping, initialization beyond the noise layer) (Section 4). Similarly, the c-model introduces many additional parameters (u_ij, b_ij in Eq. (8)) but training/regularization specifics are not provided.  
   - *Why it matters:* Performance under noisy labels can be sensitive to optimization and regularization; without these details, it is difficult to reproduce the claimed improvements (“outperforms previous methods” in Abstract; “consistently provided the best results” in CIFAR‑100).  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Provide a concise but complete training recipe per dataset/model: optimizer settings beyond “Adam defaults” (learning rate, betas if not default, weight decay), number of epochs, LR schedule, any data normalization, and for CNN the layer configuration. For c-model, specify any regularization to prevent overfitting the noise layer, and whether gradients flow through both softmaxes from the start or with a warm-up.  
   - *Essential?* Yes for a conference paper’s reproducibility standard, though code is linked; the text should still contain enough details to replicate core results.

3. **Need clearer evidence/analysis for identifiability and failure modes of the two-softmax parameterization.**  
   - *Issue:* The paper notes “degrees of freedom” and that “careful initialization… is crucial” (Section 3), but does not analyze when the model might collapse (e.g., the base classifier learns noisy labels while the noise layer becomes near-identity, or vice versa), nor does it discuss constraints/regularizers to encourage meaningful noise estimation.  
   - *Why it matters:* Jointly learning p(y|x;w) and p(z|y,x;w_noise) from only noisy labels can be non-identifiable without assumptions; initialization dependence can undermine robustness and generality.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Include diagnostics: show learned θ (or its softmax equivalent biases b_ij in s-model) vs the true injected permutation matrix; report sensitivity to initialization (uniform-noise init vs confusion-matrix init) and whether/when training diverges. Optionally add a regularizer encouraging the noise layer to be close to identity early in training (or entropy/temperature control) and ablate it.  
   - *Essential?* Important for interpretability and trustworthiness; I’d view at least the sensitivity/diagnostic plots as close to essential.

4) Minor Comments (numbered; clarity, missing details, presentation, small issues)  

1. The description of bootstrapping confidence intervals is hard to parse: “For 1000 times, N = 10 samples were randomly drawn with repeats from the N available samples” (Section 4). Please clarify what N refers to (independent training runs? different noise realizations? different subset draws?) and ensure notation is consistent.  
2. Several references to figures (Figures 1–4) are central to claims, but the text could be more self-contained by reporting at least a few representative numeric error rates at specific noise levels (e.g., p=0.4, 0.6) rather than only qualitative statements (“performed better”).  
3. Typographical/wording issues: “datsets” in Abstract; “did not not result” in Section 4; “deference” vs “difference” in Section 3.

5) Questions for Authors (3-6 questions that would clarify or strengthen the work)  
1. In the MNIST and CIFAR‑100 experiments, how many independent training runs / random seeds were used per condition, and is the bootstrapping over runs, over subsets, or over something else? (Related to the CI procedure description in Section 4.)  
2. For the c-model (Eq. (8)), what specific regularization (if any) is applied to the u_ij parameters to prevent overfitting, especially at high noise rates?  
3. How sensitive are results to the noise-layer initialization strategy described in Section 3 (uniform small noise vs confusion-matrix init)? Do you have quantitative comparisons?  
4. For CIFAR‑100, can you provide the exact CNN architecture (channels, kernel sizes, pooling) and training schedule (epochs, learning rate, weight decay, dropout)?  
5. In the synthetic corruption, the noise is via a fixed permutation. Did you test other noise structures (asymmetric confusion, uniform random flips, or instance-dependent flips) and do s-model/c-model behaviors change?

6) Recommendation  
**Weak Accept**. The core idea—optimizing the noisy-label latent-variable likelihood by augmenting the network with a noise-adaptation softmax layer (Eq. (12)–(13))—is elegant, well-motivated, and empirically promising on two benchmarks with controlled label noise, with an additional contribution on sparsifying the noise layer for scalability. The main limitations are experimental breadth (noise model realism) and missing training details (especially for CIFAR‑100/c-model), which are fixable and do not appear to invalidate the central contribution.

1) **Summary (1 paragraph; briefly describe what the paper does and its main claims/results)**  
The paper proposes learning latent binary parse trees for sentence representation using reinforcement learning, rather than relying on gold treebanks or externally predicted syntax. The sentence encoder is SPINN-style shift-reduce parsing with a TreeLSTM composition function (§2.1), while a policy network samples SHIFT/REDUCE actions and is trained with REINFORCE using downstream task performance as reward (§2.2). Across four tasks—SST sentiment, SICK relatedness, SNLI inference, and IMDB next-sentence bag-of-words generation (§3.2)—the authors claim that their “latent syntax” (unsupervised) and “semi-supervised syntax” variants outperform sequential and supervised-syntax composition baselines (§3.1, §4). They further analyze induced trees via unlabeled bracketing F1 vs Stanford Parser (≈40–42) and qualitative examples, noting an overall left-branching tendency and only partial alignment with conventional English syntax (§4).

2) **Strengths (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- **Clear, interesting problem framing:** The paper targets the gap between sequential encoders and syntax-supervised tree models by optimizing tree structure directly for downstream objectives (Abstract, §1), which is a meaningful contribution relative to prior work that either assumes trees are given (Tai et al., 2015) or supervised to match treebanks (Bowman et al., 2016).  
- **Method is concrete and implementable:** The shift-reduce setup, TreeLSTM equations (Eq. 1), validity constraints on actions, and the policy network input features ([hi, hj, xp], §2.2) are described in sufficient conceptual detail to understand the approach.  
- **Broad empirical coverage:** Evaluation spans classification (SST, SNLI), regression (SICK with MSE), and generation with a bag-of-words decoder on IMDB (§3.2), supporting the paper’s claim that the approach is generally useful rather than task-specific.  
- **Analysis beyond accuracy:** The paper reports unlabeled bracketing F1 against Stanford Parser and compares to left-/right-branching baselines (§4), plus qualitative tree examples illustrating both plausible and implausible constituents (Figures 2–3 discussion).

3) **Weaknesses (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- **Insufficient reporting of experimental results in the provided text:** The paper repeatedly refers to Tables 2–5 for key outcomes (“results in Table 2/3/4/5”), but the extracted text does not include them, leaving the magnitude of improvements and consistency across tasks unclear.  
- **Limited statistical treatment / uncertainty quantification:** Although the authors mention multiple runs (3 runs for SST; 5 for SICK, §3.2) and selecting the best run by dev performance, they do not report variance/standard deviation or confidence intervals; selecting the “best model” across restarts risks overstating gains.  
- **REINFORCE details are under-specified for reproducibility:** The RL setup states REINFORCE but omits key stabilizers and training details (e.g., baselines/variance reduction, entropy regularization, reward normalization, learning rates), which are often critical for policy gradient performance (§2.2).  
- **Baseline fairness and comparability are hard to assess:** Some baselines depend on external parses (Stanford parser, §3.1), some include “tracking LSTM” (SPINN option, §2.1) and the paper notes parameter-count confounds in SNLI (§3.2). It’s not fully clear which baselines include which components, making comparisons potentially ambiguous.

4) **Major Comments (numbered; focus on validity/significance/reproducibility; include actionable suggestions)**  

1. **Result reporting and selection protocol (essential for publication)**  
   - *Issue:* Key claims (“outperforming both sequential encoders and recursive encoders,” Abstract; “results in Table 2–5”) cannot be evaluated from the provided text because the numerical results are not shown, and the protocol selects the *best* run by dev among multiple initializations (SST: 3; SICK: 5, §3.2).  
   - *Why it matters:* Without effect sizes and uncertainty, it is hard to judge significance; best-of-N selection inflates expected test performance and can unfairly favor higher-variance methods (often the case with REINFORCE).  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Report mean ± std (or median/IQR) across seeds for each method, and avoid “best run” selection for final test reporting (or at least also report it alongside mean). If compute is limiting, use fewer methods but more robust reporting.  
   - *Essential?* Yes—at least basic aggregate statistics are needed to substantiate the central empirical claim.

2. **Policy gradient training details and variance reduction (essential for reproducibility and credibility)**  
   - *Issue:* §2.2 defines REINFORCE at a high level but does not specify whether a baseline/control variate is used, how gradients are computed with delayed reward (only at the end), whether rewards are normalized, and what optimizer/hyperparameters are used for policy vs encoder/classifier.  
   - *Why it matters:* These details can determine whether training is stable at all; policy gradient for discrete parsing decisions is notoriously high-variance. Given the paper’s stated long training times and batch size 1 (§4), training stability is a core concern.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Add explicit equations/description for the gradient estimator used (including any baseline), how reward is assigned to each action (you indicate end-only reward but still write \(\sum_t r_t\)), whether you use entropy regularization, and the learning rates/optimizers for WR vs other parameters.  
   - *Essential?* Yes—without these, replication is unlikely.

3. **Baseline specification and controlled comparisons (important, but may be addressable with clarifications)**  
   - *Issue:* The paper mixes several composition strategies (§3.1) but does not clearly state for each baseline whether it uses the same TreeLSTM composition, whether “tracking LSTM” is enabled (it is said to be optional, §2.1), and whether parameter counts are matched. The SNLI discussion explicitly suggests parameter differences can change conclusions (§3.2).  
   - *Why it matters:* Claims like “latent syntax outperforms predefined structures on all tasks” (§4) depend on fair comparisons. If some baselines lack tracking LSTM or differ in depth/parameters, improvements may not be attributable to learned structure.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* Provide a table listing, for every baseline: composition order/tree source, whether tracking LSTM is used, hidden sizes, and total parameter count. If tracking LSTM is used in left-to-right, it should also be consistently used (or consistently not used) across tree-based baselines to isolate the effect of structure learning.  
   - *Essential?* Important; at minimum clarify baseline configurations.

4. **Evaluation of induced trees: strengthen analysis beyond F1 vs Stanford and handpicked examples (non-essential but would improve significance)**  
   - *Issue:* The analysis reports unlabeled bracketing F1 ≈41–42 vs Stanford parses and notes closeness to left-branching for SNLI/SST (§4), plus a few handpicked examples (Figures 2–3 discussion).  
   - *Why it matters:* Since a key motivation is learning “hierarchical structures,” readers will want more systematic characterization: what structures are consistently learned across runs/tasks, and whether trees correlate with performance. Current evidence suggests the model may default to near-left-branching in some settings, raising the question of when genuine non-trivial trees are beneficial.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:* (i) Report distributional statistics (e.g., average tree imbalance/branching, average constituent lengths) and variance across seeds; (ii) correlate these metrics with task performance; (iii) compare induced trees across tasks to see if tasks drive different structural biases.  
   - *Essential?* Not strictly, but would substantially strengthen the paper’s broader claims.

5) **Minor Comments (numbered; clarity, missing details, presentation, small issues)**  

1. **TreeLSTM equation appears to have typos/omissions:** In Eq. 1, the output gate line uses \(b_I\) again (“\(o = \sigma(W_O[hi,hj] + b_I)\)”) and the final hidden state is written as “\(h = o\ c\)” (missing \(\tanh(c)\) and elementwise product notation) as typically used in LSTMs; please verify and correct notation.  
2. **Clarify reward notation:** You define \(R(W)=E[\sum_t r_t a_t]\) but later state reward is only observed at the end (§2.2). This is confusing as written; consider rewriting to standard REINFORCE with terminal reward applied to log-prob gradients.  
3. **Dataset construction for IMDB generation needs clearer protocol:** You say you use dev/test splits to construct train/dev/test for sentence pairs (§3.2). Please clarify whether any text from original IMDB training split is used and ensure there is no leakage between your constructed train/dev/test pair sets.

6) **Questions for Authors (3-6 questions that would clarify or strengthen the work)**  

1. Do you use any variance reduction for REINFORCE (e.g., moving-average baseline, learned critic, reward normalization, entropy bonus)? If so, where is it applied and with what coefficients?  
2. When you say “We run each model 3 times / 5 times and use the development data to pick the best model” (§3.2), do you then report the test score of the best run only, or any aggregate? Could you report mean ± std across seeds?  
3. For each baseline in §3.1, does it use the same TreeLSTM composition parameters and the tracking LSTM (§2.1), or are there architectural differences beyond composition order?  
4. In semi-supervised training (§2.2), what value of E (number of epochs) is used per dataset, and how sensitive are results to E?  
5. How exactly is the policy network trained jointly with the downstream model—same optimizer/learning rate, or separate? Are gradients from the downstream objective propagated through sampled actions in any way, or only via REINFORCE?  
6. The SNLI analysis suggests induced trees are close to left-branching (§4). Do you see tasks/datasets where induced trees are substantially different from left-branching (quantitatively), and does that correlate with larger gains?

7) **Recommendation (one of: Accept / Weak Accept / Weak Reject / Reject) + 2-3 sentence justification**  
**Weak Accept.** The idea of learning latent, task-optimized tree structures for TreeLSTM composition via reinforcement learning is novel and potentially impactful, and the paper evaluates it across multiple NLP tasks with additional tree analysis (§3–4). However, the current write-up (as provided) leaves key experimental outcomes and important RL training details under-specified, and the “best-of-N runs” reporting choice risks overstating improvements; these issues should be addressed to firmly support the central empirical claims.

1) **Summary (1 paragraph)**  
The paper presents an empirical study of adversarial example transferability on large-scale ImageNet models (ResNet-50/101/152, GoogLeNet, VGG-16) using 100 validation images correctly classified by all five models (Section 2.3). It reports that non-targeted adversarial examples generated by single-model methods (optimization-based, FG/FGS) often transfer across architectures (Section 3), whereas targeted adversarial examples generated on a single model “almost never” transfer with their target labels (Section 4). To address this, the paper proposes ensemble-based objectives for generating adversarial examples against multiple white-box models at once (Eq. (6), Section 5), showing substantially improved targeted transferability (Table 3) and near-perfect non-targeted transferability at lower reported RMSD (Table 4). The paper also provides geometric analyses (Section 6), including near-orthogonality of gradients across models and qualitative alignment of decision boundaries, and demonstrates black-box transfer to Clarifai.com with subjective semantic evaluation of targeted outcomes (Section 7).

2) **Strengths (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- Studies transferability on *large-scale* ImageNet models and multiple modern architectures (ResNets, VGG-16, GoogLeNet) rather than only MNIST/CIFAR-scale settings, aligning with the paper’s motivation in Introduction/Related Work and setup in Section 2.3.  
- Clear empirical finding that targeted transfer with target labels is rare for single-model methods (Section 4, Table 2), contrasted with relatively strong non-targeted transfer (Section 3, Table 1 discussion).  
- Ensemble-based formulation (Eq. (6)) is simple and plausible, and the “leave-one-model-out” evaluation protocol in Section 5 (generate on 4 models, test on all 5) is a reasonable way to measure transfer to an unseen model.  
- Attempts to go beyond reporting success rates by adding interpretability-oriented geometric probing (Section 6), including gradient cosine comparisons and 2D decision-plane visualizations to explain why FG struggles for targeted attacks (Table 5 discussion).

3) **Weaknesses (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- Limited evaluation scale: only 100 images are used (“randomly choose 100 images… correctly by all five models,” Section 2.3), and targeted labels are “manually choose[n]” with “semantics… far” (Section 2.3), which may introduce selection effects and makes variance/robustness unclear.  
- Several key numerical results are not present in the provided text (many are deferred to an “online technical report: Liu et al. (2016)”), including cosine similarity tables, random noise baselines, and multiple FGS/FG targeted results; this weakens self-contained reproducibility and assessment.  
- Optimization setup choices raise interpretability concerns: for single-model optimization they set λ=0 in Eq. (3) “for all experiments” (Section 3.1), effectively removing the explicit distance penalty and relying on optimization dynamics/early stopping and learning rate to control RMSD; this makes comparisons across methods less principled.  
- Clarifai.com evaluation relies on “subjective measure” of correctness and semantic closeness (Section 7) with unclear annotation protocol and no quantitative mapping between ImageNet targets and Clarifai labels, limiting the strength of the black-box targeted-claim.

4) **Major Comments (numbered; focus on validity/significance/reproducibility; include actionable suggestions)**  

1. **Dataset/target selection and statistical reliability (essential to address)**  
   - *Issue:* The main conclusions (e.g., “almost never transfer” for targeted single-model attacks; “large proportion” transfers with ensembles) are based on 100 images that are additionally filtered to be correctly classified by all five models (Section 2.3). Target labels are manually selected to be semantically far (Section 2.3), which can systematically affect targeted success rates and may not represent typical conditions. No confidence intervals or per-image variability are reported in the text.  
   - *Why it matters:* Transferability is known to be sensitive to image choice, source/target model accuracy, and target class difficulty. With n=100 and manual targets, it is hard to judge robustness, and differences between methods may not be statistically stable.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:*  
     - Report uncertainty: bootstrap confidence intervals over images for key metrics (accuracy for non-targeted; matching rate for targeted) for Tables 2–4.  
     - Provide analysis stratified by target-label “difficulty” (e.g., whether target is among top-k predictions initially, or baseline target probability). Even simple statistics like the initial target probability under each model would contextualize targeted failures/successes.  
     - Clarify and standardize target selection: define a reproducible rule (e.g., fixed mapping, random target from a disjoint semantic group, or “least-likely” class) and re-run.  
   - *Essential?* Yes, at least adding uncertainty estimates and clarifying target selection is important for validity.

2. **Optimization-based attack formulation and distortion control (important, borderline essential)**  
   - *Issue:* For the optimization-based non-targeted objective (Eq. (3)), the paper sets λ=0 for all experiments (Section 3.1), arguing Adam finds nearby adversarial examples anyway. This effectively changes the objective to purely maximizing a loss surrogate (since λd term disappears) and makes “distortion” an emergent property controlled by learning rate/iterations rather than an explicit constraint. Similarly, ensemble targeted optimization (Eq. (6)) includes λd, but the text does not state whether λ is nonzero there or how distortion is controlled beyond learning rate (“set learning rate … to be 8,” Section 5).  
   - *Why it matters:* Distortion-transfer tradeoffs are central to the claims (e.g., Table 4 claims RMSD ~17 vs ~22–23 in single-model optimization). Without explicit and comparable constraint handling, improvements might be partly due to different effective optimization regimes.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:*  
     - Report the exact λ used in Eq. (6) (and the non-targeted ensemble counterpart), and whether λ=0 is also used there.  
     - Add a controlled comparison where λ is tuned to hit matched RMSD budgets across methods (single-model vs ensemble; optimization vs FG) and compare transferability at fixed distortion.  
     - Consider reporting results under explicit norm constraints (e.g., fixed RMSD or L∞) rather than relying on learning rate, to make conclusions about “better transferability” more defensible.  
   - *Essential?* Strongly recommended; at minimum, clarify λ and provide fixed-distortion comparisons.

3. **Clarifai.com evaluation protocol and claims about targeted transfer (important but not essential)**  
   - *Issue:* Section 7 reports targeted success on Clarifai in two different senses: (i) “mislead … to predict labels irrelevant to the ground truth” (57%/76%), and (ii) “predicted as labels close to the target label” (18% ensemble vs 2% VGG), both “computed based on a subjective measure.” The protocol for judging irrelevance/closeness is not described (number of raters, criteria, blindness, inter-rater agreement), and Clarifai’s outputs are multi-label strings (Table 6) unlike ImageNet single-label targets.  
   - *Why it matters:* The black-box demonstration is a headline contribution (“first work documenting…,” Introduction/Contributions), but subjectivity and label-space mismatch make the targeted-transfer claim hard to evaluate.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:*  
     - Define a reproducible scoring rule: e.g., use WordNet similarity, embedding similarity, or a curated mapping between ImageNet targets and Clarifai tags; or at least provide rater protocol and agreement metrics.  
     - Report Clarifai top-k tags and how “close to target” was decided (e.g., any tag matching a synonym/hypernym).  
     - Separate “untargeted misclassification” from “targeted semantic steering” more clearly, since the former is easier and already covered by non-targeted transfer.  
   - *Essential?* Not strictly essential for the core ensemble-transfer result on ImageNet models, but essential to support strong “targeted black-box” claims.

5) **Minor Comments (numbered; clarity, missing details, presentation, small issues)**  

1. **Metric description wording:** In Section 2.3 “Measuring transferability,” non-targeted transferability is described as “percentage … classified correctly for the other” and called “accuracy”; consider explicitly stating that *lower is better* and that this corresponds to attack success rate = 1 − accuracy, to avoid confusion.  
2. **Duplicate abstract:** The abstract appears repeated verbatim at the top; likely a formatting issue but should be fixed in the final manuscript.  
3. **FGS transferability hypothesis incomplete:** Section 3.2.1 ends with “We hypothesize the reason why transferability of FGS is worse to this fact.” The sentence is incomplete and should be edited for clarity.

6) **Questions for Authors (3-6 questions that would clarify or strengthen the work)**  

1. What exact value(s) of λ are used in the ensemble optimization objective (Eq. (6)) for targeted and for the non-targeted counterpart? Is λ ever set to 0 as in Section 3.1?  
2. How are the “manually choose a target label… semantics is far from the ground truth” targets selected (Section 2.3)? Can you provide a deterministic rule or quantify “far” (e.g., WordNet distance)?  
3. For Tables 3 and 4, what are the per-image distributions (or at least mean±std / confidence intervals) of matching rate/accuracy across the 100 images?  
4. In Section 5, you state “In each iteration, we compute the Adam update for each model, sum up the four updates” rather than directly optimizing the stated ensemble probability in Eq. (6). Is this mathematically equivalent to optimizing Eq. (6) as written, or is it a heuristic multi-gradient aggregation?  
5. For the geometric results in Section 6, what exact gradients are used when computing cosine similarities (loss choice, targeted vs non-targeted, evaluated at x or at x*)? How stable is the “almost orthogonal” observation across images and loss definitions?  
6. For Clarifai.com evaluation (Section 7), how many annotators judged “irrelevant to ground truth” and “close to target,” and what criteria were used? Do you have agreement statistics?

7) **Recommendation: Weak Accept**  
The paper tackles an important and timely question—transferability at ImageNet scale—and the central empirical observation (targeted transfer is hard for single-model methods but becomes feasible with ensemble-based optimization, Section 5 / Table 3) is novel and potentially impactful for both attack and defense research. The main weaknesses are limited statistical grounding (n=100 with manual targets) and unclear distortion/control choices (λ=0; learning-rate-driven RMSD), plus the subjectivity of the Clarifai evaluation. These feel addressable with clearer reporting and additional summary statistics, and they do not undermine the core idea.

1) **Summary (1 paragraph; briefly describe what the paper does and its main claims/results)**  
The paper studies “identity parameterization” in deep networks through residual connections and provides both theory and experiments. Theoretically, it proves that arbitrarily deep **linear residual networks** of the form \((I+A_\ell)\cdots(I+A_1)\) have (within a bounded-norm region) **no non-global critical points** (Theorem 2.2), supported by an existence result that there is always a global optimum with per-layer spectral norm shrinking like \(O(1/\ell)\) when depth is large enough (Theorem 2.1). For **nonlinear residual networks** with single-ReLU residual blocks, it proves a **finite-sample memorization / expressivity** theorem: under a mild separation assumption (Assumption 3.1), a residual network can fit any labeling with \(O(n\log n + r^2)\) parameters (Theorem 3.2). Empirically, inspired by the simplicity of the expressivity construction, it evaluates an “all-convolutional residual network” without batch norm/dropout/max-pool and reports improved or competitive results on CIFAR10/100 and improved over prior all-convolutional baselines on ImageNet, while also noting underfitting on ImageNet relative to standard ResNet-34.

2) **Strengths (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- **Clear and strong optimization statement for linear residual parameterization:** Theorem 2.2 establishes that for any \(\tau<1\), every critical point in \(B_\tau=\{|||A|||\le\tau\}\) is global, and the proof is transparent via the gradient factorization in Lemma 2.4 and the “cannot cancel identity” argument.  
- **Helpful existence/conditioning perspective:** Theorem 2.1 connects depth \(\ell\) with the existence of small-norm global optima \(|||A^*|||\lesssim (\sqrt{\pi}+\sqrt{3\gamma})^2/\ell\), where \(\gamma\) depends on \(\sigma_{\min}(R),\sigma_{\max}(R)\), giving an interpretable “deeper ⇒ smaller per-layer change” message.  
- **Concrete finite-sample expressivity construction:** Theorem 3.2 provides an explicit parameter-count scaling \(O(n\log n+r^2)\) using single-ReLU residual blocks \(h\mapsto h+V\mathrm{ReLU}(Uh+s)\) (Eq. 3.1), backed by Lemma B.1’s selective update mechanism (Eq. 3.6).  
- **Empirical architecture simplification is well-motivated by theory:** The experimental model deliberately removes batch norm/dropout/max pool and uses near-zero initialization (Section 4.1), aligning with the “identity-friendly” framing and producing competitive CIFAR10 error (6.38% vs 6.43% cited from He et al. (2015)).

3) **Weaknesses (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- **Theorem 2.2 is local-to-a-region and does not guarantee training iterates stay there:** The key landscape claim holds inside \(B_\tau\) for \(\tau<1\), and the paper explicitly notes that staying in \(B_\tau\) “is not guaranteed by Theorem 2.2 by itself” (discussion around Eq. 2.3). This limits the direct algorithmic implication.  
- **Finite-sample expressivity relies on assumptions and randomness that are not fully quantified:** Assumption 3.1 requires \(\|x^{(i)}-x^{(j)}\|\ge \rho\) and the proof uses JL plus “random unit vectors \(q_j\)” being “w.h.p.” uncorrelated; however, explicit probability bounds / constants and how they scale with \(n,r,\rho\) are mostly informal (e.g., “technically, if \(k>4\log r\)…”).  
- **Experimental reporting is incomplete for reproducibility and fair comparison:** While optimizer/momentum, batch size, weight decay, learning rate schedule, and initialization variance are given (Section 4.1/4.2), key details are missing from the provided text: exact architecture table contents (“Table 1” not included), exact CIFAR/ImageNet comparison numbers (“Table 2” not included), augmentation specifics beyond “standard,” and the exact implementation changes relative to the referenced TF ResNet codebase.  
- **Claims of “improves significantly” are hard to evaluate without variance/statistics:** The paper reports single numbers (e.g., 6.38% CIFAR10, 35.29% ImageNet top-1) without standard deviation across runs or confidence intervals, which is especially relevant since the method emphasizes initialization and removal of batch norm.

4) **Major Comments (numbered; focus on validity/significance/reproducibility; include actionable suggestions)**  

1. **Scope and algorithmic implications of Theorem 2.2 (critical points in \(B_\tau\))**  
   - *Issue:* The main optimization result (Theorem 2.2) is conditioned on \(|||A|||\le \tau<1\). Yet the training dynamics for either gradient descent on population risk or SGD on empirical risk are not shown to remain in \(B_\tau\). Eq. (2.3) gives a PL-type inequality in \(B_\tau\), but the “in-domain” assumption is the central missing step for turning the landscape result into an optimization guarantee.  
   - *Why it matters:* Without a mechanism ensuring iterates stay in the region where the landscape is benign, the result may be more of a “local benignness” statement than an “optimization is easy” guarantee, especially since residual layers in practice can leave the small-norm regime.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:*  
     - Provide a sufficient condition (even if conservative) on step size / initialization / regularization under which gradient descent iterates stay within \(B_\tau\) for the linear model (2.1) on population risk (or empirical risk).  
     - Alternatively, empirically demonstrate (for the linear setting) that with near-zero initialization the iterates indeed remain in a small-norm region and converge to global optimum, and contrast with standard parameterization \(A_\ell\cdots A_1\).  
   - *Essential?* Strengthens significance and interpretation; not strictly required for correctness of Theorem 2.2, but important for the “optimization becomes easy” narrative.

2. **Relationship between population-risk theory and finite-sample/empirical training**  
   - *Issue:* Section 2 analyzes population risk under a linear-Gaussian noise model (Eq. 2.2, Claim 2.3), while Section 3 proves perfect fitting of finite samples under separation (Assumption 3.1), and Section 4 reports classification results. The conceptual bridge—how these results inform training behavior in realistic nonlinear CNNs—is mostly qualitative (“Directly inspired…”) and the conclusion conjectures an extension to nonlinear single-ReLU residuals.  
   - *Why it matters:* For an ML conference contribution, the coherence of the overall story matters: readers will ask what concrete, testable predictions the linear landscape theory makes for the nonlinear CNN experiments (beyond general “identity is good”).  
   - *Actionable suggestions:*  
     - Add an ablation in Section 4 that more directly tests the theory-inspired levers: e.g., (i) compare near-zero initialization vs standard He init under the same architecture; (ii) compare single-ReLU residual block vs standard two-ReLU/two-BN residual block while controlling parameter count.  
     - Explicitly discuss which parts of Theorem 2.2 are expected to transfer (e.g., “no cancellation of identity” intuition) and which do not (ReLU nonlinearity, batch statistics, convolutional structure).  
   - *Essential?* Not essential for correctness, but important for strengthening the paper’s central thesis connecting theory ↔ practice.

3. **Reproducibility and completeness of experimental specification**  
   - *Issue:* The paper references “Table 1” architecture and “Table 2” results, but in the provided text the actual layer-by-layer configuration and full benchmark comparisons are not visible. Even within the prose, several key choices are underspecified: exact data augmentation pipeline (“standard data augmentation”), whether label smoothing/EMA is used (not mentioned), how the fixed random projection last layer is constructed (distribution, normalization), and details of padding/avg-pool when strides change (“as is standard”).  
   - *Why it matters:* The empirical claim is that this *simpler* architecture “improves significantly” on prior all-convolutional nets across CIFAR10/100/ImageNet; to validate this, others must be able to replicate the exact model/training.  
   - *Actionable suggestions:*  
     - Provide a fully explicit architecture listing in text (not only in a table), including number of blocks per stage, channel widths, kernel sizes, and where striding occurs.  
     - Specify the random last-layer projection (seeded? fixed at init? Gaussian? scaled?), and how logits/probabilities are computed from it.  
     - Report multiple runs (at least for CIFAR) with mean±std, since initialization is highlighted as crucial (“Gaussian noise mainly as a symmetry breaker”; different σ than standard conv init).  
   - *Essential?* Important for empirical validation; likely required for acceptance if the empirical improvement is a key part of the contribution.

5) **Minor Comments (numbered; clarity, missing details, presentation, small issues)**  

1. **Notation/typos and small clarity issues:** There are multiple minor extraction/typo artifacts (e.g., “gradients of vanish” near Section 1.1; inconsistent \(Id\) vs \(Idd\); some index typos like “σmin(Id +A>i )” in the proof). A careful proofreading pass would improve readability.  
2. **Assumption 3.1 realism and scope:** The text says images can be “imperceptibly perturbed” to satisfy separation; it would help to briefly discuss whether the expressivity construction is stable to such perturbations or purely existential.  
3. **ImageNet training details:** Section 4.2 mentions asynchronous updates across 6 machines; please specify whether this is async SGD with parameter server, and any details about weight decay, augmentation, and whether batch size is global or per worker (it states per machine batch size 256, but readers will want the global effective batch).

6) **Questions for Authors (3-6 questions that would clarify or strengthen the work)**  

1. For Theorem 2.2 / Eq. (2.3): do you have a concrete condition (step size / initialization radius / explicit regularizer) under which gradient descent on the *empirical* risk stays within \(B_\tau\) with \(\tau<1\)?  
2. In Section 4.1, what exactly is the “fixed random projection” last layer: its distribution, scaling, and whether it is followed by softmax with cross-entropy? Is it re-sampled per run?  
3. How sensitive are CIFAR results (e.g., the reported 6.38% CIFAR10) to the proposed smaller initialization \(\sigma=1/(k^2 c)\) versus standard conv initialization? Do you have an ablation?  
4. Theorem 3.2 uses random \(A_0\) and random surrogate label vectors \(q_j\). What are the explicit high-probability guarantees and required \(k\) as a function of \(n,r,\rho\) (beyond the stated \(k= c(\log n)/\rho^2\) and “if \(k>4\log r\)”)—can you state a single consolidated bound?  
5. In Section 4.2, you report underfitting on ImageNet. Did you try increasing width/depth while keeping the “no BN/dropout/maxpool” constraint, or is underfitting mainly attributed to optimization (learning-rate schedule) rather than capacity?

7) **Recommendation (one of: Accept / Weak Accept / Weak Reject / Reject) + 2-3 sentence justification**  
**Weak Accept.** The paper offers a notably clean and meaningful theoretical contribution: the “no spurious critical points in a small-norm region” result for deep linear residual networks (Theorem 2.2) is stronger than prior linear-network landscape results and the proof is compelling and well-grounded (Lemma 2.4 → Theorem 2.2). The finite-sample expressivity theorem (Theorem 3.2) and the simplified all-conv residual experiments are interesting, but the experimental section as provided lacks enough detail/statistics to fully substantiate the “improves significantly” claims; addressing reproducibility/ablations would solidify the overall contribution.

1) **Summary (1 paragraph; briefly describe what the paper does and its main claims/results)**  
This paper proposes **Bidirectional Generative Adversarial Networks (BiGANs)** to learn an *inverse mapping* from data to latent space within the GAN framework by jointly training a generator \(G: \Omega_Z \to \Omega_X\) and an encoder \(E: \Omega_X \to \Omega_Z\) against a discriminator that distinguishes joint pairs \((x,E(x))\) from \((G(z),z)\) (Eq. (2)–(3)). The authors provide theory paralleling classical GAN results: with an optimal discriminator, the objective reduces to a Jensen–Shannon divergence between joint distributions \(P_{EX}\) and \(P_{GZ}\) (Proposition 2), and at the global optimum these joint distributions match (Theorem 1), implying \(E\) and \(G\) are inverses almost everywhere (Theorem 2). Empirically, they evaluate feature learning by transferring the learned encoder to supervised tasks, reporting competitiveness on **permutation-invariant MNIST** and **ImageNet/PASCAL VOC transfer**; they also compare to baselines using a GAN discriminator as features and a “latent regressor” trained on generated samples (Section 4.1), and show qualitative reconstructions \(G(E(x))\) on ImageNet (Section 4.3, Figure 4 referenced).

2) **Strengths (3-4 bullet points; specific, paper-grounded)**  
- **Clear, elegant objective formulation**: the discriminator operates on joint tuples \((x,z)\) and the minimax objective (Eq. (2)–(3)) is a natural extension of GANs that directly targets representation learning rather than only generation.  
- **Strong theoretical grounding**: the paper derives the **optimal discriminator** via Radon–Nikodym derivatives (Proposition 1) and shows the game minimizes **\(D_{JS}(P_{EX}\,\|\,P_{GZ})\)** (Proposition 2), culminating in an inversion guarantee **\(G(E(x))=x\)** and **\(E(G(z))=z\)** a.e. at optimum (Theorem 2).
